verses.commentary: Pharisees and Sadducees — unlikely allies (theologically opposed groups). United only by hostility to Jesus. They test him, asking for a sign from heaven.
Weather observation: red sky at evening, fair weather coming. Common forecasting wisdom.
Red and stormy sky in morning, rain coming. They read meteorological signs but not 'signs of the times' (semeia ton kairon, σημεῖα τῶν καιρῶν). Categorical blindness.
'Wicked and adulterous generation seeks a sign' — repetition of 12:39. Only the sign of Jonah given. Jesus leaves them — abandonment as judgment.
Disciples cross the lake — forget to bring bread. Practical oversight.
'Beware the leaven (zyme, ζύμη) of the Pharisees and Sadducees.' Image of pervasive influence. Doctrine spreads like yeast.
Disciples assume Jesus is rebuking their bread-failure. Literal misreading of a metaphor.
Jesus knows. 'Little-faith, why discuss having no bread among yourselves?' He asks rhetorically — the issue is not bread.
'Do you not yet understand?' Recalls the 5000: how many baskets gathered? Memory of provision should preclude bread-anxiety.
And the 4000 — how many baskets? Two miracle-events should have established trust in provision. The disciples' slowness exposed.
'How can you not understand I was not talking about bread?' Jesus restates the metaphor. Watch out for the leaven — the teaching.
Then they understand: not literal leaven but the teaching (didache, διδαχή) of Pharisees and Sadducees. Doctrine spreads pervasively, must be guarded against.
Caesarea Philippi — Roman city with pagan temples, including to Caesar Augustus. Imperial cult territory. The question 'Who do men say I am?' is asked in idol-thick air.
Disciples report popular guesses: John the Baptist (Herod's theory), Elijah (forerunner), Jeremiah (suffering prophet), one of the prophets. All wrong but reaching toward greatness.
'But you — who do you say I am?' The pivotal question. Personal confession demanded.
Peter answers: 'You are the Christ (the Messiah), the Son of the living God.' Full confession — both Messiah and divine sonship. Climax of disciples' Christology so far.
'Blessed are you, Simon Bar-Jonah' — formal patronymic. 'Flesh and blood did not reveal this — but my Father in heaven.' Confession is gift, not insight.

'You are Peter (Petros, Πέτρος, stone), and on this rock (petra, πέτρα, bedrock) I will build my church (ekklesia, ἐκκλησία).' Word play. The 'rock' debated: Peter himself, his confession, or Christ. Gates of Hades will not prevail. The church's resilience is structural.

Keys of the kingdom given to Peter — authority symbol (Isaiah 22:22). Binding and loosing (deo and luo, δέω and λύω) — rabbinic for declaring forbidden or permitted, exercising authoritative decision. Earthly action ratified in heaven.
Strict warning: tell no one he is the Christ. The messianic secret continues. Confession must mature through the cross before being broadcast.

From this point Jesus begins explaining: he must (dei, δεῖ) go to Jerusalem, suffer at elders' hands, be killed, raised on the third day. The passion-prediction pattern begins (the first of three: 16:21; 17:22-23; 20:18-19).
Peter takes him aside and rebukes (epitimao, ἐπιτιμᾶν — the same word used for rebuking demons): 'Far be it from you, Lord! This will never happen.' The just-confessor now corrects the Christ.

'Get behind me, Satan!' (hypage opiso mou, satana). The same one called Rock now called adversary. Peter has become a stumbling stone (skandalon, σκάνδαλον) — thinking the things of men, not of God. The cross is the dividing line.

'If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself, take up his cross, and follow me.' Discipleship reformulated post-passion-prediction. Self-denial, cross-bearing, following — three present-tense verbs.
Save your life, lose it. Lose your life for my sake, find it. Paradox repeated (compare 10:39). Self-preservation is self-destruction.
Gain whole world, forfeit soul (psyche, ψυχή — life/self). What ransom would suffice for the soul? Cost-benefit analysis settles in eternity's favor.
Son of Man will come in Father's glory with angels, repay each according to deeds. Final judgment, behavioral basis. Discipleship has consequences that endure.
Some standing here will not taste death before seeing the Son of Man coming in his kingdom. Likely refers to the transfiguration in the next chapter — a preview of glory granted before death.
